functions.name: C++ concurrency toolbox quick reference
functions.signature:
/* C++ concurrency toolbox

   Thread lifetime
     std::thread                 : explicit join() or detach() required (C++11)
     std::jthread                : joins automatically; stop token support (C++20)

   Protect shared state
     std::mutex + lock_guard     : exclusive RAII lock
     std::unique_lock            : movable lock, deferred locking, condition variables
     std::scoped_lock            : lock several mutexes safely (C++17)
     std::shared_mutex           : several readers or one writer (C++17)

   Wait for events
     std::condition_variable     : block until a predicate changes
     std::counting_semaphore     : count available permits (C++20)
     std::latch                  : one-shot countdown gate (C++20)
     std::barrier                : reusable phase rendezvous (C++20)

   Return asynchronous results
     std::async                  : launch callable and obtain std::future
     std::promise                : manually publish a value or exception
     std::packaged_task          : wrap a callable and retrieve its future
     std::shared_future          : several readers for the same result

   Lock-free shared state
     std::atomic<T>              : atomic load/store/read-modify-write operations
     atomic::wait / notify_*     : efficient value-change waiting (C++20)
*/
functions.insertText:
/* C++ concurrency toolbox

   Thread lifetime
     std::thread                 : explicit join() or detach() required (C++11)
     std::jthread                : joins automatically; stop token support (C++20)

   Protect shared state
     std::mutex + lock_guard     : exclusive RAII lock
     std::unique_lock            : movable lock, deferred locking, condition variables
     std::scoped_lock            : lock several mutexes safely (C++17)
     std::shared_mutex           : several readers or one writer (C++17)

   Wait for events
     std::condition_variable     : block until a predicate changes
     std::counting_semaphore     : count available permits (C++20)
     std::latch                  : one-shot countdown gate (C++20)
     std::barrier                : reusable phase rendezvous (C++20)

   Return asynchronous results
     std::async                  : launch callable and obtain std::future
     std::promise                : manually publish a value or exception
     std::packaged_task          : wrap a callable and retrieve its future
     std::shared_future          : several readers for the same result

   Lock-free shared state
     std::atomic<T>              : atomic load/store/read-modify-write operations
     atomic::wait / notify_*     : efficient value-change waiting (C++20)
*/
functions.description: Quick selection guide for the standard C++ concurrency toolbox.
functions.longDescription: Quick selection guide for the standard C++ concurrency toolbox.
functions.declaration:
/* C++ concurrency toolbox

   Thread lifetime
     std::thread                 : explicit join() or detach() required (C++11)
     std::jthread                : joins automatically; stop token support (C++20)

   Protect shared state
     std::mutex + lock_guard     : exclusive RAII lock
     std::unique_lock            : movable lock, deferred locking, condition variables
     std::scoped_lock            : lock several mutexes safely (C++17)
     std::shared_mutex           : several readers or one writer (C++17)

   Wait for events
     std::condition_variable     : block until a predicate changes
     std::counting_semaphore     : count available permits (C++20)
     std::latch                  : one-shot countdown gate (C++20)
     std::barrier                : reusable phase rendezvous (C++20)

   Return asynchronous results
     std::async                  : launch callable and obtain std::future
     std::promise                : manually publish a value or exception
     std::packaged_task          : wrap a callable and retrieve its future
     std::shared_future          : several readers for the same result

   Lock-free shared state
     std::atomic<T>              : atomic load/store/read-modify-write operations
     atomic::wait / notify_*     : efficient value-change waiting (C++20)
*/

functions.name: choose std::thread std::jthread or std::async
functions.signature:
/* Which abstraction should I choose?

   std::thread
     Use when you must explicitly own a thread and control join/detach.

   std::jthread (C++20)
     Prefer for owned worker threads: destructor requests stop when possible and joins.

   std::async + std::future
     Use for task-oriented code when you want a result or exception back.
     Pass std::launch::async when a separate asynchronous execution is required.

   Framework event loop
     For GUI timers and callbacks, prefer the framework scheduler (for example QTimer)
     instead of blocking the UI thread.
*/
functions.insertText:
/* Which abstraction should I choose?

   std::thread
     Use when you must explicitly own a thread and control join/detach.

   std::jthread (C++20)
     Prefer for owned worker threads: destructor requests stop when possible and joins.

   std::async + std::future
     Use for task-oriented code when you want a result or exception back.
     Pass std::launch::async when a separate asynchronous execution is required.

   Framework event loop
     For GUI timers and callbacks, prefer the framework scheduler (for example QTimer)
     instead of blocking the UI thread.
*/
functions.description: Decision guide for thread ownership versus asynchronous tasks.
functions.longDescription: Decision guide for thread ownership versus asynchronous tasks.

/* Which abstraction should I choose?

   std::thread
     Use when you must explicitly own a thread and control join/detach.

   std::jthread (C++20)
     Prefer for owned worker threads: destructor requests stop when possible and joins.

   std::async + std::future
     Use for task-oriented code when you want a result or exception back.
     Pass std::launch::async when a separate asynchronous execution is required.

   Framework event loop
     For GUI timers and callbacks, prefer the framework scheduler (for example QTimer)
     instead of blocking the UI thread.
*/

functions.name: synchronization primitive selection quick reference
functions.signature:
/* Synchronization primitive selection

   Protect an invariant              : std::mutex
   Lock several mutexes              : std::scoped_lock (C++17)
   Many readers, occasional writer   : std::shared_mutex (C++17)
   Wait for state change             : std::condition_variable
   Count available resources         : std::counting_semaphore (C++20)
   Release workers once              : std::latch (C++20)
   Synchronize repeated phases       : std::barrier (C++20)
   Initialize once                   : std::call_once + std::once_flag
   Wait for atomic value change      : std::atomic<T>::wait / notify_* (C++20)
*/
functions.insertText:
/* Synchronization primitive selection

   Protect an invariant              : std::mutex
   Lock several mutexes              : std::scoped_lock (C++17)
   Many readers, occasional writer   : std::shared_mutex (C++17)
   Wait for state change             : std::condition_variable
   Count available resources         : std::counting_semaphore (C++20)
   Release workers once              : std::latch (C++20)
   Synchronize repeated phases       : std::barrier (C++20)
   Initialize once                   : std::call_once + std::once_flag
   Wait for atomic value change      : std::atomic<T>::wait / notify_* (C++20)
*/

functions.name: future promise packaged_task async quick reference
functions.signature:
/* Asynchronous result selection

   std::async
     Call a function now and retrieve the result later through std::future.

   std::promise
     Producer manually chooses when to publish a value or exception.

   std::packaged_task
     Wrap a callable, enqueue or run it elsewhere, then retrieve its future.

   std::future
     Single consumer. get() retrieves the result once.

   std::shared_future
     Copyable shared result for several consumers.
*/
functions.insertText:
/* Asynchronous result selection

   std::async
     Call a function now and retrieve the result later through std::future.

   std::promise
     Producer manually chooses when to publish a value or exception.

   std::packaged_task
     Wrap a callable, enqueue or run it elsewhere, then retrieve its future.

   std::future
     Single consumer. get() retrieves the result once.

   std::shared_future
     Copyable shared result for several consumers.
*/

   std::async

functions.name: future shared state lifecycle reference
functions.signature:
/* Future shared-state lifecycle

   Producer side
     std::async                 creates task + shared state
     std::promise               manually stores value or exception
     std::packaged_task         stores callable result or exception

   Consumer side
     std::future                movable single-consumer handle
     std::shared_future         copyable multi-consumer handle

   Notes
     future::get() retrieves the result and invalidates a std::future.
     shared_future::get() can be called by several consumers.
     worker exceptions are observed when get() is called.
*/
functions.insertText:
/* Future shared-state lifecycle

   Producer side
     std::async                 creates task + shared state
     std::promise               manually stores value or exception
     std::packaged_task         stores callable result or exception

   Consumer side
     std::future                movable single-consumer handle
     std::shared_future         copyable multi-consumer handle

   Notes
     future::get() retrieves the result and invalidates a std::future.
     shared_future::get() can be called by several consumers.
     worker exceptions are observed when get() is called.
*/
functions.description: Reference for the lifecycle of futures and their shared state.
functions.longDescription: Reference for the lifecycle of futures and their shared state.

/* Future shared-state lifecycle

   Producer side
     std::async                 creates task + shared state
     std::promise               manually stores value or exception
     std::packaged_task         stores callable result or exception

   Consumer side
     std::future                movable single-consumer handle

   Notes
     future::get() retrieves the result and invalidates a std::future.

*/

functions.name: parallel algorithm callback safety checklist
functions.signature:
/* Parallel algorithm callback checklist

   - Do not write the same object from multiple invocations without synchronization.
   - Avoid hidden global state and order-dependent side effects.
   - With par_unseq or unseq, do not use operations that cannot be safely interleaved.
   - Benchmark: parallel dispatch overhead can dominate small workloads.
   - Verify the implementation and toolchain support for parallel backends.
*/
functions.insertText:

   - Benchmark: parallel dispatch overhead can dominate small workloads.
   - Verify the implementation and toolchain support for parallel backends.
*/

functions.name: producer consumer shutdown sequence
functions.signature:
/* Producer-consumer shutdown sequence

   1. Under the queue mutex, set closed = true.
   2. notify_all() waiting consumers.
   3. Each consumer exits when closed && queue.empty().
   4. Join all owned workers.
   5. Release resources only after workers have stopped.

   Do not destroy a mutex, condition_variable, queue, or captured object while a
   worker can still access it.
*/
functions.insertText:
/* Producer-consumer shutdown sequence

   1. Under the queue mutex, set closed = true.
   2. notify_all() waiting consumers.
   3. Each consumer exits when closed && queue.empty().
   4. Join all owned workers.
   5. Release resources only after workers have stopped.

   Do not destroy a mutex, condition_variable, queue, or captured object while a
   worker can still access it.
*/
functions.description: Reference for orderly shutdown of producer-consumer workers.
functions.longDescription: Reference for orderly shutdown of producer-consumer workers.

functions.name: coroutine quick reference C++20
functions.signature:
/* Coroutine quick reference (C++20)

   co_await expression : suspend until an awaitable operation is ready
   co_yield expression : publish a value and suspend
   co_return value     : complete the coroutine

   A coroutine is not automatically a new thread. It is a resumable function.
   The return type and its promise_type define allocation, suspension and result
   handling. Frameworks and libraries usually provide ready-to-use task types.
*/

functions.name: coroutine is not a thread reference
functions.signature:
/* Coroutine versus thread

   Coroutine
     - resumable function state machine
     - suspension is cooperative
     - does not imply parallel execution
     - typically scheduled by a framework or event loop

   Thread
     - operating-system execution context
     - can run concurrently on another core
     - requires synchronization for shared mutable data

   Use a library task type or framework integration rather than writing a custom
   promise_type for production code unless the ownership model is documented.
*/
functions.insertText:

*/

functions.name: standard timer design reference
functions.signature:
/* C++ timing quick reference
   Relative blocking delay       : std::this_thread::sleep_for(duration)
   Absolute blocking deadline    : std::this_thread::sleep_until(timePoint)
   Elapsed time / timeout clock  : std::chrono::steady_clock
   Calendar timestamp clock      : std::chrono::system_clock
   Signaled wait with timeout    : std::condition_variable::wait_for / wait_until
   Managed cancellable worker    : std::jthread + std::stop_token (C++20)

   The standard library does not provide a universal event-loop timer object.
   GUI frameworks such as Qt and operating systems expose their own event timers.
*/
functions.insertText:
/* C++ timing quick reference
   Relative blocking delay       : std::this_thread::sleep_for(duration)
   Absolute blocking deadline    : std::this_thread::sleep_until(timePoint)
   Elapsed time / timeout clock  : std::chrono::steady_clock
   Calendar timestamp clock      : std::chrono::system_clock
   Signaled wait with timeout    : std::condition_variable::wait_for / wait_until
   Managed cancellable worker    : std::jthread + std::stop_token (C++20)

   The standard library does not provide a universal event-loop timer object.
   GUI frameworks such as Qt and operating systems expose their own event timers.
*/
functions.description: Choose the appropriate timing primitive for the required behavior.
functions.longDescription: This reference clarifies which standard primitive matches each timing need. For GUI applications, prefer the framework event loop timer, such as QTimer, rather than blocking the UI thread.

functions.name: C++ text formatting family guide
functions.signature: std::format / std::print / stream formatting navigation guide
functions.declaration: std::format / std::print / stream formatting navigation guide
functions.insertText: // Prefer std::format for string construction, std::print/std::println for direct output, and iostream manipulators for stream state.
functions.description: Navigation guide for modern C++ text formatting and stream-oriented output.
functions.longDescription:
Choose the formatting family according to the destination:

- `std::format` and `std::vformat`: construct formatted strings.
- `std::format_to` and `std::format_to_n`: write through an output iterator.
- `std::print` and `std::println`: write formatted text directly when C++23 `<print>` is available.
- iostream manipulators such as `std::setw`, `std::setprecision`, `std::hex`, and `std::fixed`: modify stream state.
- `std::stringstream`: parse or assemble text through stream operations.

functions.name: Reference — DllMain loader-lock restrictions
functions.signature: /* DllMain must remain minimal. Defer complex initialization outside the loader lock. */
functions.insertText: /* DllMain must remain minimal. Do not call LoadLibrary or LoadLibraryEx from DllMain. Avoid synchronization with other threads, process creation, COM initialization, User32/GDI calls, and other loader-lock-sensitive work. Prefer explicit Initialize / Shutdown exports or lazy initialization. */
functions.description: Reference note for DllMain loader-lock restrictions and deferred initialization.
functions.longDescription: Reference note for DllMain loader-lock restrictions and deferred initialization.

functions.name: Reference — LoadLibrary aliases and GetProcAddress variants
functions.signature: /* LoadLibrary and LoadLibraryEx are encoding-neutral aliases. GetProcAddress has one API and accepts either an ANSI export name or an ordinal. */
functions.insertText: /* LoadLibrary and LoadLibraryEx select A or W according to UNICODE. Prefer explicit W variants in Unicode Windows code. GetProcAddress has no W variant: exported PE symbol names are resolved with LPCSTR or with MAKEINTRESOURCEA(ordinal). Pair each successful LoadLibrary* call with FreeLibrary unless lifetime is intentionally pinned. */
functions.description: Reference note for encoding-neutral aliases and GetProcAddress lookup modes.
functions.longDescription: Reference note for encoding-neutral aliases and GetProcAddress lookup modes.
functions.declaration: /* LoadLibrary and LoadLibraryEx are encoding-neutral aliases. GetProcAddress has one API and accepts either an ANSI export name or an ordinal. */

functions.name: Reference — LoadLibrary aliases and GetProcAddress variants
functions.signature: /* LoadLibrary and LoadLibraryEx are encoding-neutral aliases. GetProcAddress has one API and accepts either an ANSI export name or an ordinal. */
functions.insertText: /* LoadLibrary and LoadLibraryEx select A or W according to UNICODE. Prefer explicit W variants in Unicode Windows code. GetProcAddress has no W variant: exported PE symbol names are resolved with LPCSTR or with MAKEINTRESOURCEA(ordinal). Pair each successful LoadLibrary* call with FreeLibrary unless lifetime is intentionally pinned. */
functions.description: Reference note for encoding-neutral aliases and GetProcAddress lookup modes.
functions.longDescription: Reference note for encoding-neutral aliases and GetProcAddress lookup modes.
functions.declaration: /* LoadLibrary and LoadLibraryEx are encoding-neutral aliases. GetProcAddress has one API and accepts either an ANSI export name or an ordinal. */
